title: Refute-or-Promote: An Adversarial Stage-Gated Multi-Agent Review Methodology for High-Precision LLM-Assisted Defect Discovery
problem: LLM defect/vulnerability discovery produces too many plausible-but-wrong reports that overwhelm maintainers
unit_of_analysis: Candidate defect report on real targets
intervention: Adversarial multi-stage review with cross-model critic and empirical validation gates
method: Stratified context hunting + adversarial kill mandates + Cross-Model Critic (different LLM families) + mandatory empirical validation gates
data: 31-day field eval over 7 targets (security libs / ISO C++ / compilers); external acceptance (4 CVEs etc)
main_claim: External adversarial review filters ~79-83% of false positives before disclosure
relation_to_ours: VERIFIED ANCHOR / closest prior work. Shares cross-model critique for code defects (problem+method overlap). DIFFERS from ours: no strict equal-budget comparison vs author extra-compute; no full bidirectional author<->reviewer matrix; no defect-type-level error-correlation model; no pre-call routing; precision/external-acceptance metric not final patch correctness under matched budget.

title: Cross-Model LLM Code Review: Should you use Claude to review Codex or vice versa?
venue_or_status: ResearchGate / reported Agentic SE @ KDD '26 workshop
problem: Whether to pair different LLMs as writer vs reviewer for code
unit_of_analysis: Code produced by one model reviewed by another (writer/reviewer/reviser roles)
method: Reported writer/reviewer/reviser pipeline with roles assigned to different models (AO=Claude-written/Codex-reviewed vs OA reverse)
data: Reported comparison of collaboration configs; cross-model review ranks #2 by evaluators
main_claim: CLOSEST POTENTIAL TWIN on the bidirectional cross-model code-review axis (writer<->reviewer order, Claude<->Codex). COULD NOT FETCH (HTTP 403 on ResearchGate; no arXiv/OpenReview mirror found) -- UNVERIFIED, not counted as a confirmed neighbor. If real it threatens difference (2) (bidirectional matrix). Our likely surviving deltas even vs it: strict equal-budget matching vs author extra-compute, defect-type-level error correlation, and pre-call routing on held-out repositories.

title: Agent-as-a-Router: Agentic Model Routing for Coding Tasks
authors: Pengfei Zhou, Zhiwei Tang, Yixing Ma, Jiasheng Tang, et al.
problem: Routing a coding task to the most suitable LLM among many providers
unit_of_analysis: Task-dimension performance statistics across frontier LLMs
intervention: Agentic router (Context->Action->Feedback loop) that accumulates execution-grounded experience
method: ACRouter: Orchestrator + Verifier + Memory; closes information deficit during deployment
main_claim: verified scores from 8 frontier LLMs); in/out-of-distribution regret
relation_to_ours: Lowest cumulative regret in-distribution; generalizes OOD
collision_risk: NEAREST NEIGHBOR for difference (4) (pre-call routing) on code. Shares: routing across heterogeneous models exploiting complementary strengths; OOD generalization. DIFFERS: routes WHICH model GENERATES, not whether/which model REVIEWS an existing patch; no author<->reviewer review action; no strict budget matching vs author extra-compute; no defect-type error-correlation; routing target set is single-model selection, not {no-review/self/same-family/cross-family/test-gen/reimplementation}.

title: Phase Transition for Budgeted Multi-Agent Synergy
authors: Bang Liu, Linglong Kong, Jian Pei
problem: Predicting when multi-agent systems help vs saturate vs fail under a fixed inference budget
unit_of_analysis: Hierarchical agent trees with correlated performance
intervention: Theory of budgeted synergy with closed-form compute-allocation rules
method: Proves sharp phase transition in b-ary trees from communication fidelity
main_claim: and fan-in; derives routing condition s>beta
collision_risk: Budgeted synergy occurs exactly when organization exponent s exceeds scaling exponent beta
column16: HIGH conceptual overlap: directly ties WHEN-collaboration-helps to ERROR CORRELATION under a BUDGET -- our core thesis in theory form. DIFFERS: abstract/theoretical (synthetic + generic agents), not code or coding agents; error correlation is a scalar tree parameter, not per-defect-type author-reviewer correlation; no bidirectional code author<->reviewer matrix; no held-out-repository empirical router; no patch-correctness endpoint. Strong citation for our budget+correlation framing; our empirical code-specific instantiation survives.

title: Single-Agent LLMs Outperform Multi-Agent Systems on Multi-Hop Reasoning Under Equal Thinking Token Budgets
problem: Multi-agent gains are confounded by extra test-time compute
unit_of_analysis: Multi-hop reasoning tasks across 3 model families
intervention: Budget-controlled (equal reasoning-token) comparison of single- vs multi-agent systems
method: Information-theoretic (Data Processing Inequality) argument + controlled empirical comparison across 5 MAS architectures
data: FRAMES and MuSiQue; Qwen3 / DeepSeek-R1 / Gemini 2.5
main_claim: Single-agent matches/beats multi-agent at equal tokens unless context utilization degrades
relation_to_ours: NEAREST NEIGHBOR for difference (1) (strict budget matching) -- same skeptical 'normalize compute and the multi-agent advantage disappears' move. DIFFERS: domain is multi-hop reasoning, not code review/repair; compares MAS-vs-SAS topology, not author<->reviewer cross-model review; no defect-type error correlation; no routing; budget unit is thinking tokens not author-extra-compute vs second-model cost. Validates our methodology stance; we extend it to coding-agent review with a complementarity router.

title: Correlated Errors in Large Language Models
authors: Elliot Kim, Avi Garg, Kenny Peng, Nikhil Garg
problem: Whether diversity in training/architecture/provider actually reduces shared mistakes
unit_of_analysis: Individual model performance on shared tasks
intervention: Large-scale measurement of cross-model error agreement
data: Two leaderboards + a resume-screening task; 350+ LLMs
main_claim: Errors are substantially correlated (60% agreement when both err); correlation grows with accuracy even across providers
relation_to_ours: FOUNDATION for difference (3): empirically establishes that heterogeneous models are NOT automatically diverse in errors -- the premise our complementarity-not-heterogeneity thesis depends on. DIFFERS: not code, not code review; measures population-level error correlation, not author<->reviewer per-defect-type correlation conditioned on a patch; no budget matching; no review intervention; no routing. Cite as motivation; our defect-type, author-reviewer-conditioned correlation in coding agents is novel.

title: Articulate but Wrong: Self-Review Failures in LLM-Based Code Modernization
authors: Gokul Chandra Purnachandra Reddy, Aditya Lolla, Harsha Sanku
problem: Can a model recognize when its own code change silently alters behavior
method: 1980 modernization calls across 11 LLMs (7 families); type-strict behavioral oracle
data: 60-snippet legacy Python-2 corpus with semantic traps
main_claim: Behavioral drift in 39.7% of trap snippets; 31.7% silently self-endorsed; failures cluster across models (r=0.52)
relation_to_ours: Supports our self-review baseline weakness AND the cross-model error-correlation argument (failures cluster, r=0.52). DIFFERS: narrow task (Py2->3 modernization), not general patch review; same-model self-review only (no bidirectional cross-model matrix); detection-only, no final-patch-correctness under matched budget; no routing; correlation is across-model snippet clustering, not per-defect-type author<->reviewer complementarity.

title: Cross-Context Review: Improving LLM Output Quality by Separating Production and Review Sessions
problem: Same-session self-review fails to catch a model's own errors
unit_of_analysis: Reviews of artifacts with injected errors (incl. code/docs)
intervention: Compare same-session vs fresh-session vs subagent review
column17: Context separation (not repetition) drives the gain; CCR F1 28.6% > same-session 24.6%
column18: Sharpens our SELF vs SAME-FAMILY baseline design (isolates context-separation as a confound we must control). DIFFERS: single-model only, no cross-model bidirectional matrix; tiny synthetic injected-error set, not repository patches with hidden tests; no budget matching; no defect-type error correlation; no routing. Useful baseline-design citation; our cross-model complementarity + budget + routing contribution is untouched.

title: LLM Critics Help Catch LLM Bugs
authors: Nat McAleese, Rai Michael Pokorny, Juan Felipe Ceron Uribe, et al. (OpenAI)
problem: Humans struggle to evaluate model-written code
intervention: Train an LLM critic (RLHF) to write NL feedback on code
method: Critic models fine-tuned via RLHF; compared to human reviewers
data: Real assistant code tasks + ChatGPT training data
main_claim: Model critiques beat human critiques in 63% of cases and find more bugs
relation_to_ours: BOUNDARY paper: establishes 'LLM critics can find code bugs' -- which our novelty boundary forbids us from claiming. DIFFERS: same-family critique, no heterogeneous bidirectional matrix; no equal-budget comparison vs author extra-compute; no defect-type error correlation; no routing; detection quality endpoint, not budget-matched final patch correctness. Cite to scope-out the generic claim.

title: Code Review Agent Benchmark (c-CRAB)
authors: Yuntong Zhang, Zhiyuan Pan, Imam Nur Bani Yusuf, Haifeng Ruan, Ridwan Shariffdeen, Abhik Roychoudhury
problem: Can code-review agents catch issues humans would catch in auto-generated code
intervention: Benchmark code-review agents against human-derived ground truth
method: Dataset built from human reviews with generated tests as held-out validation
data: c-CRAB dataset; evaluates PR-agent / Devin / Claude Code / Codex
main_claim: Agents solve only ~40% of c-CRAB; agent reviews target different aspects than humans
relation_to_ours: Provides a candidate evaluation substrate and notes human/agent COMPLEMENTARITY (a motif we formalize). DIFFERS: benchmark only, no causal author<->reviewer matrix across model families; no budget matching; no defect-type error-correlation model; no routing; human-vs-agent framing rather than model-vs-model complementarity. Possible data/eval baseline to cite and differentiate.

title: Sphinx: Benchmarking and Modeling for LLM-Driven Pull Request Review
authors: Daoan Zhang, Shuo Zhang, Zijian Jin, Jiebo Luo, Shengyu Fu, Elsie Nallipogu
problem: Automating PR review under noisy supervision and weak metrics
unit_of_analysis: PR review comments and code changes
intervention: Data pipeline + checklist benchmark + reward-based training (CRPO)
method: Pseudo-modified vs merged code data generation; checklist reward policy optimization
main_claim: Up to 40% checklist-coverage gains; SOTA review completeness/precision
relation_to_ours: Code-review benchmarking+training neighbor; shows the PR-review task and metrics landscape. DIFFERS: single-model training-focused, no cross-model bidirectional matrix; no budget matching vs author extra-compute; no defect-type author<->reviewer error correlation; no pre-call routing; checklist-coverage metric, not budget-matched final patch correctness. Cite for task framing/metrics; low overlap on our four deltas.

title: LLM Consortium for Software Design Refinement: A Controlled Experiment on Multi-Agent Collaboration Topologies
problem: Which multi-agent topology best supports software design refinement
intervention: Factorial controlled experiment manipulating Authority x Roles x Dynamics
data: 8 design tasks x 5 reps; GPT-OSS / Claude Opus / Claude Sonnet evaluators
main_claim: Structural adversarial topologies rank #1; cross-model review ranks #2; parallel-merge poor
relation_to_ours: Methodologically similar controlled comparison of review/collaboration topologies (incl. cross-model review) for code/software work. DIFFERS: software ARCHITECTURE DESIGN, not defect patches with hidden tests; rubric-score metric, not patch correctness; no strict budget matching; no defect-type error correlation; no pre-call routing; ranks topologies but does not predict per-case when to call a second model. Good 'controlled multi-agent comparison' precedent to cite and surpass.

title: Cross-Model Disagreement as a Label-Free Correctness Signal
problem: Detecting confident errors without ground-truth labels
intervention: Use a second (verifier) model's surprise as a correctness signal
method: Cross-Model Perplexity / Cross-Model Entropy via single forward pass
main_claim: Cross-model signals beat within-model uncertainty (AUROC 0.75 vs 0.59 on MMLU); mentions routing applications
relation_to_ours: Relevant to 'when does a second model add value' (difference 4) -- uses cross-model disagreement, and explicitly lists model-routing as an application. DIFFERS: reasoning/QA, not code or patch review; passive uncertainty signal, not an author<->reviewer review action producing a corrected patch; no budget matching; no defect-type error correlation; routing only mentioned, not built/evaluated on held-out repositories. Cite for the disagreement-as-signal idea; our intervention+routing+budget contribution survives.
